subject: dermatology
decision: Merged all 3 student-recalled sittings into ONE recall bank; sitting/year labels intentionally dropped per past-paper guardrail.

questions.q: Norwegian (crusted) scabies is classically associated with:
questions.explain: Crusted (Norwegian) scabies = huge mite burden with thick hyperkeratotic crusts, seen in the immunosuppressed (HIV/AIDS, elderly, steroids). Highly contagious; needs oral ivermectin + topical scabicide.

questions.q: Nail pitting is most characteristically seen in:
questions.explain: Psoriasis causes nail pitting, oil-drop (salmon) patches, onycholysis and subungual hyperkeratosis. Pitting also occurs in alopecia areata.

questions.q: Onycholysis (nail plate separating from the bed) is a classic sign of:
questions.explain: Psoriasis commonly causes onycholysis (distal separation of the plate from the nail bed), along with pitting and subungual hyperkeratosis. Fungal infection is another cause.

questions.q: The characteristic cutaneous/vascular sign of systemic sclerosis is:
questions.explain: Raynaud phenomenon (episodic white→blue→red digital colour change) is usually the earliest sign of systemic sclerosis; other clues: sclerodactyly, telangiectasia, calcinosis, perioral furrowing, microstomia, beaked nose (CREST).

questions.q: A typical adverse effect of long-term potent topical corticosteroids is:
questions.explain: Chronic topical steroids cause skin atrophy, striae (stretch marks), telangiectasia, easy bruising, hypopigmentation, perioral dermatitis and tachyphylaxis.

options: Tender red nodules on the shins
questions.q: Erythema nodosum most characteristically presents as:
questions.explain: Erythema nodosum = tender, red, ill-defined nodules on the anterior shins (a septal panniculitis). Causes: streptococcal infection, primary TB, sarcoidosis, IBD, drugs (sulfonamides, OCP), pregnancy.

questions.q: Nikolsky's sign (skin sloughs with lateral pressure) is positive in:
questions.explain: Nikolsky positive = pemphigus vulgaris, SJS/TEN, staphylococcal scalded skin. It is negative in bullous pemphigoid (sub-epidermal, tense blisters).

options: Periungual fibromas (Koenen tumours), ash-leaf macules, adenoma sebaceum
questions.q: Which finding suggests tuberous sclerosis?
questions.explain: Tuberous sclerosis skin signs: ash-leaf hypomelanotic macules, facial angiofibromas (adenoma sebaceum), shagreen patch, periungual (Koenen) fibromas. Distractor 'Crowe sign' (axillary freckling) belongs to neurofibromatosis-1.

questions.q: Seborrheic keratosis is best described as:
questions.explain: Benign keratinocytic tumour with a 'stuck-on', warty, brain-like surface; dermoscopy shows milia-like cysts and comedo-like openings. Eruptive onset = Leser-Trélat sign.

questions.q: Psoriasis can be associated with:
questions.explain: Psoriasis is associated with psoriatic arthritis and systemic comorbidities: metabolic syndrome, cardiovascular disease, obesity, depression, IBD, uveitis.

questions.q: The treatment of squamous cell carcinoma is:
questions.explain: Invasive SCC → surgical excision with clear margins (Mohs for high-risk sites); nodal/imaging staging if needed.

questions.q: Which HPV types are responsible for genital warts (condylomata)?
questions.explain: Low-risk HPV 6 and 11 cause anogenital warts. High-risk 16 and 18 drive cervical/oropharyngeal SCC. The 9-valent vaccine covers 6/11 + 7 oncogenic types.

questions.q: In which disease is the papule the main (primary) lesion?
questions.explain: Lichen planus = the 5 Ps: Purple, Polygonal, Pruritic, Planar Papules, with Wickham striae. Papule = solid elevation <0.5 cm.

questions.q: Which is NOT a risk factor for developing squamous cell carcinoma?
questions.explain: Metabolic syndrome is not an SCC risk factor. SCC risks: chronic UV, immunosuppression, scars/chronic wounds (Marjolin), HPV, radiation, arsenic.

questions.q: Norwegian scabies is associated with:
questions.explain: Crusted (Norwegian) scabies occurs with immunosuppression (HIV/AIDS), huge mite loads and hyperkeratotic crusting.

questions.q: The adverse effect of topical corticosteroids highlighted in the exam is:
questions.explain: Striae + skin atrophy, telangiectasia, bruising, perioral dermatitis are classic topical-steroid side-effects.

questions.q: In a first (primary) scabies infestation, signs/itching appear after about:
questions.explain: Primary infestation → pruritus after ~4–6 weeks (sensitisation). Re-infestation → symptoms within 24–48 h.

questions.q: Bullous pemphigoid is:
questions.explain: BP is the commonest autoimmune blistering disease, typically in the elderly; tense sub-epidermal blisters, Nikolsky negative, anti-BP180/230.

questions.q: The blister in pemphigus vulgaris is:
questions.explain: PV = intra-epidermal, suprabasal acantholytic split (anti-DSG3). Contrast: pemphigoid = sub-epidermal.

questions.q: Squamous cell carcinoma most commonly metastasizes first to:
questions.explain: Cutaneous SCC spreads first to regional lymph nodes. Metastatic risk rises for lesions on mucosa, scars/burns, and in immunosuppression.

questions.q: A result of chronic (cumulative) radiation/UV exposure is:
questions.explain: Chronic UV/radiation → actinic keratosis (SCC precursor). SCC has the strongest association with cumulative sun exposure.

questions.q: The vascular sign of systemic scleroderma is:
questions.explain: Raynaud's phenomenon — often the first sign of systemic sclerosis.

options: Motor nerve involvement/paralysis (e.g. Ramsay-Hunt, segmental weakness)
questions.q: Herpes zoster can cause:
questions.explain: Beyond the painful dermatomal rash, VZV reactivation can cause motor involvement (segmental paresis, Ramsay-Hunt facial palsy, ophthalmic zoster). Commonest complication = post-herpetic neuralgia.

options: A tender red plaque with silvery scale
A pearly nodule with telangiectasia and a rolled (pearly-papule) border
questions.q: Basal cell carcinoma (nodular type) classically appears as:
questions.explain: Nodular BCC = a pearly/translucent nodule with arborising telangiectasia, rolled edge with pearly papules at the periphery, often ulcerated ('rodent ulcer'). Dermoscopy: arborising vessels, blue-grey ovoid nests, leaf-like areas.

questions.q: Tuberous sclerosis — which is a genuine sign? a) Auspitz b) freckles c) periungual fibroma d) Crowe sign
questions.explain: Periungual (Koenen) fibromas are a tuberous-sclerosis feature. Crowe sign (axillary freckling) is a distractor — it belongs to neurofibromatosis-1.

questions.q: The most common type of alopecia in the white population is:
questions.explain: Androgenetic alopecia is the commonest hair loss (higher incidence in white populations): progressive, non-scarring, symmetrical, androgen-dependent, genetically predisposed.

questions.q: The alopecia associated with a psychological cause is:
questions.explain: Trichotillomania = compulsive hair-pulling (often children, right temporal region in right-handers). Non-scarring, hairs broken at varying lengths, normal scalp.

questions.q: Perioral furrowing and microstomia are seen in:
questions.explain: Systemic sclerosis: skin tightening → perioral radial furrowing, microstomia (small mouth), beaked nose, sclerodactyly, salt-and-pepper pigmentation.

questions.q: Itching (pruritus) is a MAJOR criterion of which condition?
questions.explain: In the Hanifin–Rajka criteria, pruritus is an essential (major) criterion of atopic dermatitis — the itch-scratch cycle drives the disease.

questions.q: Which nail change is linked to psoriasis rather than lichen planus (onycholysis question)?
questions.explain: Onycholysis (with pitting, oil-drop sign) → psoriasis. Lichen planus nails → longitudinal ridging, pterygium, thinning.

questions.q: Which is NOT part of Hutchinson's triad?
questions.explain: Hutchinson's triad (congenital syphilis) = notched teeth + interstitial keratitis + VIII-nerve deafness. Neurosyphilis is not included.

options: Primary tuberculosis (and strep, sarcoid, IBD, drugs)
questions.q: Erythema nodosum is associated with:
questions.explain: EN causes: streptococcal infection, primary TB, sarcoidosis, IBD, drugs (sulfonamides, OCP), pregnancy, Behçet. It is NOT classically linked to rheumatoid arthritis.

questions.q: Gottron's papules are seen in:
questions.explain: Gottron's papules (violaceous papules over knuckles) + heliotrope rash + shawl/V-sign = dermatomyositis. Adult DM: screen for underlying malignancy.

questions.q: The most common form of skin cancer is:
questions.explain: Basal cell carcinoma is the commonest skin cancer — locally invasive, very rarely metastasises.

questions.q: Which drug is NOT used in acne treatment?
questions.explain: Cyclosporine is not used for acne (can induce it). Acne: topical retinoids, benzoyl peroxide, azelaic acid, antibiotics, hormones, isotretinoin.

questions.q: Erythema nodosum is NOT typically associated with:
questions.explain: EN links to strep, TB, sarcoidosis, IBD, drugs and pregnancy. Rheumatoid arthritis is not a classic EN trigger (it was the exam distractor).

questions.q: What is NOT caused by streptococci?
questions.explain: Furuncle is a Staphylococcus aureus perifollicular abscess. Streptococci cause erysipelas, non-bullous impetigo, scarlet fever, cellulitis.
